title: Add append-only progress log for Queen crash recovery
description:
Root cause: If the Queen crashes mid-workflow (context limit, timeout, API error), the next session has no structured way to determine what completed and what didn't. The user must manually inspect session artifacts, git log, and beads status to reconstruct state. This is slow, error-prone, and blocks efficient session resumption.

Fix: Give the Queen a single new responsibility — append one line to a progress log after each workflow milestone. The log is write-only during normal operation (no reads, no file opens — just echo >>). Recovery sessions read it once to determine the resume point.

File location: {session-dir}/progress.log

Format: TIMESTAMP | STEP_NAME | key=value pairs

Milestone entries (one per workflow step):
- SCOUT_COMPLETE — metadata dir path, task count, blocked count
- PANTRY_IMPL_COMPLETE — prompt count, skeleton count, file paths
- DIRT_PUSHER_COMPLETE — one entry per dirt-pusher with task ID, status (success/failed), summary path or error
- REVIEW_SLOTS_FILLED — prompts dir path
- NITPICKER_TEAM_SPAWNED — member count
- BIG_HEAD_CONSOLIDATED — report path
- PC_CHECKPOINT_VERDICT — pass/fail, details
- BEADS_FILED — count
- SESSION_CLOSE_STARTED — (marks landing-the-plane initiated)

Design properties:
- Zero read cost during normal operation: Queen never opens the file to read, only appends
- Negligible context cost: one echo >> per milestone, not per tool call
- Recovery is a single read: next session reads the log once to find last completed step
- Artifact paths embedded in each entry so recovery session knows where to find intermediate results
- Human-readable format (not JSON) for quick manual inspection

Recovery workflow (manual for now, automated recovery is a separate future issue):
- New session checks for {session-dir}/progress.log
- Reads last entry to determine resume point
- User/Queen decides whether to resume or start fresh

Affected files:
- orchestration/RULES.md — Add progress log append after each workflow step
- orchestration/templates/scout.md — No change (Queen appends after Scout returns)
- bd prime or session startup — No change for now (manual recovery)

Acceptance criteria:
1. Queen appends exactly one log entry after each workflow milestone listed above
2. Log is append-only — Queen never reads or overwrites the file during normal operation
3. Each entry includes enough context (paths, counts, status) to determine resume point
4. Progress log is written to {session-dir}/progress.log
5. Format is human-readable with pipe-delimited fields

title: Parallelize review prompt composition with implementation via bash scripts
description:
Root cause: Review prompt composition (Pantry Section 2) currently runs as a separate LLM subagent invocation after implementation finishes, adding unnecessary latency. The review prompts are formulaic — they follow rigid templates with variable slots for commit range, changed files, and task IDs. This work is mechanical text assembly, not compositional reasoning, yet it consumes a full LLM round-trip.

Fix: Split review prompt composition into two bash scripts called at different workflow stages:

Script 1 — skeleton assembly (called by Pantry during Section 1):
- Reads reviews.md and nitpicker-skeleton.md templates
- Assembles 4 review data files + Big Head consolidation file with slot markers ({{COMMIT_RANGE}}, {{CHANGED_FILES}}, {{TASK_IDS}}, {{TIMESTAMP}})
- Writes review skeletons to {session-dir}/review-skeletons/
- Pantry verifies output files exist and have correct structure
- Pantry returns skeleton paths alongside implementation prompt paths

Script 2 — slot filling (called by Queen after dirt-pushers finish):
- Takes session dir, commit range, changed file list, task IDs, timestamp as arguments
- Runs sed/envsubst to fill slot markers with actual values
- Writes final review prompts to {session-dir}/prompts/
- Writes combined previews to {session-dir}/previews/
- Returns exit code only — Queen never reads file contents

Impact on Queen context: zero. Queen passes paths to Nitpicker team without opening files. Only an error message reaches the Queen if a script fails.

Affected files:
- pantry.md — Add Step 1.5 after impl prompt composition: call Script 1, verify output
- pantry.md Section 2 — Remove or mark as deprecated (replaced by scripts)
- RULES.md — Update post-implementation step to call Script 2 instead of spawning review Pantry
- scripts/ — New: compose-review-skeletons.sh, fill-review-slots.sh

Acceptance criteria:
1. Pantry Section 1 invocation produces both implementation prompts AND review skeletons
2. No second Pantry invocation is needed for review prompt composition
3. Queen context window absorbs zero template content from review prompt composition
4. Review prompts produced by the scripts are identical in structure to current Pantry Section 2 output
5. Script failures surface as error messages to the Queen, not silent failures

title: Big Head failure artifact timestamp coexistence under re-spawn not documented
description: The Big Head failure artifact filename is review-consolidated-{TIMESTAMP}-FAILED.md, where {TIMESTAMP} is Queen-provided. If Big Head is re-spawned after failure, a new timestamp would be provided, so the old failure artifact and new success artifact coexist (correct by design). However, this dependency on fresh timestamps at re-spawn is not documented. Affected surfaces: big-head-skeleton.md:58, big-head-skeleton.md:20. Fix: No code change needed. Add a comment noting that both -FAILED and success files may coexist after a retry cycle, and that re-spawn requires a fresh TIMESTAMP value.

title: Missing <IF ROUND 1> markers on placeholder guard path list in reviews.md
description: The ant-farm-l3d5 fix added a placeholder substitution guard at orchestration/templates/reviews.md:519-537 that iterates over all 4 review report paths (correctness, edge-cases, clarity, excellence) to detect unsubstituted template tokens. However, the <IF ROUND 1> / </IF ROUND 1> markers were only applied to the clarity/excellence entries in the polling while-loop body (lines 553-556), NOT to the guard's for-loop (lines 520-524). This asymmetry means Pantry may leave clarity/excellence paths with unsubstituted tokens in the guard, triggering a false-positive PLACEHOLDER_ERROR=1 and exit 1 in round 2+ sessions. Fix: Add <IF ROUND 1> / </IF ROUND 1> markers around lines 523-524 in the guard's for-loop, matching the convention used in the while-loop body.

title: Dual placeholder conventions for round-conditional blocks

Fix: Standardize on one marker convention. Add Pantry self-check for leftover markers. Acceptance criteria: (1) One consistent marker convention. (2) Pantry validates composed briefs for leftover markers. Found by: Excellence (EX-1, EX-5 both P3).

title: dirt-pusher-skeleton.md bd close ordering creates failure window before summary doc
description:

The instruction is ambiguous about when it executes relative to Step 6 (write summary doc). If an agent closes the bead before writing the summary doc and then crashes, the task is marked closed with no summary artifact. Checkpoint B (checkpoints.md:246) requires reading the summary doc and would find nothing, but the task is already closed.

Affected surfaces:
- dirt-pusher-skeleton.md:42 -- 'After committing: bd close {TASK_ID}' appears orphaned after step list

Fix: Move 'bd close {TASK_ID}' to an explicit position after Step 6 with a note: 'Close the bead ONLY after the summary doc is successfully written.' Consider making it Step 7.

Acceptance criteria:
- bd close instruction is explicitly sequenced after summary doc write (Step 6)
- Either incorporated into a numbered step or clearly labeled as post-Step-6
- No ambiguity about when bead closure occurs relative to artifact creation

title: Strengthen IF ROUND 1 markers from interpretive to executable
description: The <IF ROUND 1> / </IF ROUND 1> markers in reviews.md are bash comments interpreted by an LLM (Big Head/Pantry) rather than executed by a script. title: parse-progress-log.sh bash/zsh incompatibility breaks crash recovery on macOS
description: Root cause: scripts/parse-progress-log.sh uses declare -A (bash 4+ associative arrays) but ships with #\!/usr/bin/env bash. On macOS, bash 3.2 (no associative array support) and zsh (different associative array semantics) are the available shells. The script's crash recovery function is completely broken on the primary target platform. Bash 3.2: declare -A fails with exit code 2, which RULES.md Step 0 interprets as 'session completed' — recovery silently skipped. Zsh: declare -A runs but all array lookups fail silently — all steps show as pending, resume always starts at step 0. Affected files: scripts/parse-progress-log.sh:25,113-115,130-133,179. Fix: Add bash version guard (exit 1 on bash < 4) or rewrite step detection using POSIX-compatible arrays/grep. Acceptance criteria: (1) Script exits with clear error on bash < 4 (not exit code 2), OR script works on bash 3.2 and zsh. (2) All step detection, completion check, and resume point determination produce correct results on macOS. Found by: Edge Cases (EC-1 P1, EC-2 P1), Correctness (CR-7 P1), Excellence (EX-3 P3). Severity conflict: P1 vs P3 (2-level gap) — flagged for calibration review.

title: Delay Big Head bead filing until after Pest Control checkpoint validation
description:
Root cause: Big Head currently files beads for review findings before CCD and DMVDC checkpoints run. If checkpoints identify invalid findings (hallucinated, misattributed, doesn't match actual diff), those beads already exist and require manual cleanup. This is wasted work and pollutes the issue tracker with unvalidated findings.

Fix: Add Pest Control to the Nitpicker team and gate bead filing on checkpoint validation. Big Head consolidates and writes the report as usual but holds off on bd create calls until Pest Control confirms the findings pass checkpoints.

Revised team composition: 4 Nitpickers + Big Head + Pest Control (6 members, still one team)

Flow:
1. 4 Nitpickers produce review reports
2. Big Head consolidates, deduplicates, writes consolidated report to {session-dir}/review-reports/review-consolidated-{timestamp}.md
3. Big Head does NOT file beads yet — messages Pest Control: 'consolidated report ready at {path}'
4. Pest Control runs CCD and DMVDC checkpoints against the consolidated report and actual code/diffs
5. Pest Control messages Big Head with verdict:
   - PASS: all findings validated
   - FAIL: list of specific findings that failed validation, with reasons
6. On PASS: Big Head files beads for all findings
7. On FAIL: Big Head flags the failures to the Queen with specifics (which findings failed, why). User decides whether to drop, adjust, or re-review. Big Head files beads only for the validated findings.

Key design decisions:
- Big Head stays alive in the team waiting for PC's verdict — no re-invocation needed
- PC communicates via team messaging (SendMessage), not file-based handoff
- Queen's context window is not impacted — no report content enters the Queen's window
- On checkpoint failure, Big Head escalates to the Queen rather than silently adjusting or dropping findings

Affected files:
- orchestration/templates/reviews.md — Update Big Head Consolidation Protocol to add checkpoint gate before bead filing
- orchestration/templates/big-head-skeleton.md or agents/big-head.md — Add wait-for-PC step between consolidation and bead filing
- orchestration/RULES.md — Update review team composition to include Pest Control (6 members)
- orchestration/templates/pantry.md Section 2 — Big Head data file needs to reference the PC coordination step

Acceptance criteria:
1. Big Head does not file any beads until Pest Control confirms checkpoint validation
2. Pest Control is spawned as part of the Nitpicker team, not as a separate Queen-orchestrated agent
3. On checkpoint pass, all consolidated findings are filed as beads
4. On checkpoint fail, only validated findings are filed as beads; failures are escalated to Queen with specifics
5. No review report content enters the Queen's context window during this process

title: Pre-commit hook silently allows PII when scrub script not executable
description: Root cause: The generated pre-commit hook in install-hooks.sh:72-75 checks if scrub-pii.sh is executable and exits 0 (allows commit) with only a WARNING if it's not. This means a missing or non-executable scrub script silently allows PII-containing issues.jsonl into git history. Additionally, install-hooks.sh does not chmod +x scrub-pii.sh after installation. Fix: Either (1) change exit 0 to exit 1 in the non-executable path to block commits when PII protection unavailable, or (2) add 'chmod +x scripts/scrub-pii.sh' to install-hooks.sh. Acceptance criteria: (1) PII cannot enter git history when scrub script is missing/non-executable. (2) install-hooks.sh ensures scrub-pii.sh is executable after installation. Found by: edge-cases-reviewer (P2), clarity-reviewer (P3).

fix: CCO artifact naming uses session-wide format in practice but checkpoints.md specifies per-task
description:
checkpoints.md:179 specifies per-task CCO artifact naming: `pc-{TASK_SUFFIX}-cco-{timestamp}.md` with example at line 28: `pc-74g1-cco-20260215-001145.md`. In practice, both observed sessions use session-wide naming: `pc-session-cco-{timestamp}.md` because the Queen batches all wave prompts into one CCO run.

Root cause: The CCO specification was written assuming one CCO run per task. In practice, the Queen batches all prompts from a wave into a single CCO audit, producing one session-scoped artifact rather than per-task artifacts. The RULES.md Hard Gates table (line 258) uses a wildcard `*-cco-*.md` that accommodates both patterns — the gate works regardless, but the naming spec is misleading.

Current behavior: A future Pest Control agent following checkpoints.md literally would write per-task files. Anyone querying pc/ for `*{TASK_SUFFIX}-cco-*` will find nothing. The wildcard gate still matches, so no runtime failure, but the naming convention is documented incorrectly.

Fix: Update checkpoints.md CCO section to document that batched CCO runs use `pc-session-cco-{timestamp}.md`. Keep per-task naming as valid for single-task CCO runs. Update the example on line 28.

Changes needed:
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md:179: Add batch naming variant
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md:28: Update example to show both patterns

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] checkpoints.md documents both per-task and session-wide CCO naming patterns
- [ ] Example on line 28 reflects actual practice
- [ ] Naming convention matches actual artifacts in recent sessions

description: Root cause: The sed substitution at scripts/compose-review-skeletons.sh:108 and :163 uses regex [A-Z][A-Z_]* to convert ALL {UPPERCASE_WORD} tokens to {{UPPERCASE_WORD}} slot markers. No allowlist limits which tokens are converted. If template prose uses {UPPERCASE_WORD} as display text (e.g. in code examples), it would be silently corrupted into a double-brace slot marker. Additionally, the inline comment claims the regex matches '2+ chars' but [A-Z][A-Z_]* matches 1+ chars (the + should be used instead of *). title: Automated Queen crash recovery from progress log
description:
Root cause: Even with a progress log (see prerequisite issue), crash recovery is manual — the user or next Queen must read the log, interpret the state, and decide what to resume. This is feasible but adds friction and requires the user to understand the workflow steps.

Fix: Add automated recovery logic that reads the progress log on session startup and presents a structured resume plan. This is the foundation for eventual supervisor-level orchestration.

Recovery logic:
1. On session startup (via bd prime or startup hook), check for {session-dir}/progress.log files in recent session dirs
2. If an incomplete log is found (no SESSION_CLOSE_STARTED entry), parse the last completed step
3. Present a resume plan to the user:
   - What completed (with artifact paths)
   - What was in progress (may need re-run)
   - What hadn't started yet
   - Recommended resume point
4. User approves or chooses to start fresh

This is a stepping stone toward a supervisor agent that could spawn Queens and recover them automatically, but that is out of scope for this issue.

Affected files:
- orchestration/RULES.md or bd prime hook — Add progress log detection on startup
- Potentially a new script: scripts/parse-progress-log.sh

Acceptance criteria:
1. Incomplete progress logs are detected on session startup
2. A structured resume plan is presented showing completed/in-progress/pending steps
3. User can approve resume or choose fresh start
4. No action is taken automatically — user must approve the resume plan

title: [HALF-BAKED] Group co-located tasks into single Dirt Pusher assignments
description:
## Idea

When multiple tasks touch the same file(s), the Scout should group them and assign the group to a single Dirt Pusher instead of spawning separate agents. This avoids git rebase conflicts, gives the agent full context of related changes, and reduces cost/agent count.

The Scout already has the File Modification Matrix (Step 4) that identifies which tasks touch which files — the grouping logic itself is straightforward.

## Motivation

Currently the framework assumes 1 agent = 1 task. When two tasks both modify the same file, they're either serialized into different waves (slow) or run concurrently and risk rebase conflicts. Grouping them into one agent assignment eliminates both problems.

## Blast Radius

This is NOT a small change. The 1-agent-1-task assumption is baked into 6+ components:

| Component | Current assumption | What breaks |
|---|---|---|
| Scout (Step 5) | Wave groupings list task IDs | Needs a "task group" concept — groups of co-located tasks assigned to one agent |
| Pantry | One verdict table row per task, one task brief per agent | Needs to compose combined briefs for task groups |
| Dirt Pusher skeleton | Single {TASK_ID}, single bd close, single summary doc | Needs to iterate: claim all, implement all, close all, write one summary covering multiple tasks |
| Pest Control — DMVDC | Verifies one task's acceptance criteria per agent | Needs to verify multiple tasks' criteria against one agent's commits |
| Pest Control — WWD | Scope check against one task's file list | Scope check against union of grouped tasks' file lists |
| RULES.md | Agent count = task count per wave | Agent count < task count; needs group-aware counting |
| Coverage verification | Counts tasks assigned to waves | Counts tasks assigned to groups assigned to waves |
| Reviews / Big Head | Commit-based, task-agnostic | Minimal impact — reviews look at diffs, not task assignments |

## Key Design Decisions (unresolved)

- Should grouping be mandatory or advisory (Scout recommends, Queen decides)?
- Max tasks per group? (unbounded grouping could overload a single agent)
- How does the summary doc work — one per group or one per task?
- What if grouped tasks have different priorities? (P1 + P3 in same group)
- Does the single-task path need to remain as a separate code path, or can a group of 1 be the universal case?

title: Pass 2: Consolidate batch outputs into final triage report
description:
Scope: Merge all 9 Pass 1 batch outputs plus Pass 0 duplicate data into a single triage report for user review.

Input files:
- .beads/agent-summaries/_session-39adef65/audit/pass1-batch-{A..I}-output.json (9 files, ~168 entries total)
- .beads/agent-summaries/_session-39adef65/audit/pass0-exact-dupes.json (16 duplicate pairs)
- .beads/agent-summaries/_session-39adef65/audit/pass0-epics-skip.json (8 epic IDs)

Process:
1. Load all 9 batch output files and verify each is valid JSON
2. Verify total entry count = 168 (176 beads minus 8 epics)
3. Build duplicate cluster map: start with 16 exact-dupe pairs from Pass 0, add DUPLICATE_SUSPECT findings from Pass 1, merge transitive duplicates into clusters
4. For one-sided duplicate suspects (only one agent flagged it), mark as "needs human review"
5. Aggregate verdicts into sections, organized for efficient user review

Output: Write the final report to .beads/agent-summaries/_session-39adef65/audit/final-triage-report.md

Report sections:
1. Executive Summary — counts by verdict, net reduction from 176
2. Confirmed Duplicates — clusters with recommended keep/close, grouped by confidence (mechanical vs agent-identified)
3. Already Fixed — beads to close, with evidence citations, grouped by file area
4. Irrelevant — beads to close, with reasons
5. Still Valid — beads to keep, organized by epic parent
6. Needs Human Review — low-confidence verdicts, one-sided duplicate suspects, NEEDS_CONTEXT beads
7. Priority Re-calibration — any beads where agents suggested a different priority
8. Epic Health — per-epic summary showing children still valid vs closeable and percentage reduction

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Report accounts for all 176 beads (168 verified + 8 epics noted)
- [ ] No bead ID is missing from the report
- [ ] Duplicate clusters are fully resolved (no orphaned one-sided suspects without human review flag)
- [ ] Executive summary math is correct (sum of all sections = 176)
- [ ] Still Valid section is organized by epic for easy scanning
- [ ] Report is valid markdown with consistent table formatting

Add explicit scope fencing to Nitpicker agent definitions per review type
description:
Root cause: All four Nitpicker reviewers (clarity, edge-cases, correctness, excellence) share a single agent MD file with no review-type-specific identity. Differentiation comes entirely from the Pantry-composed data files. This means each reviewer has no baked-in understanding of what is NOT its responsibility, leading to duplicate findings across reviewers that Big Head must deduplicate after the fact.

The most impactful gap is the absence of 'not my job' boundaries — each reviewer can stray into another's territory because nothing in its agent definition fences its scope.

Fix: Add per-review-type specialization blocks to the Nitpicker agent definition(s). For each review type, define:
- Explicit 'NOT your responsibility' list referencing the other three review types
- Type-specific heuristics and patterns to look for (what good findings look like for this type)
- Type-specific severity calibration (e.g., missing null check is P1 for edge-cases, not reportable for clarity)

This can be implemented as either:
(a) Four separate agent MD files sharing a common structure, each with a specialization section, OR
(b) A single agent MD file with conditional sections keyed to a review type variable injected by the data file

Option (b) is preferred for maintainability — keeps report format, output structure, and messaging guidelines in one place while still providing scope fencing. The data file would include a REVIEW_TYPE marker that activates the relevant specialization block.

Affected files:
- agents/nitpicker.md (or ~/.claude/agents/nitpicker.md) — Add per-type specialization blocks with scope fences, heuristics, and severity calibration
- orchestration/templates/reviews.md — May need to reference the new scope boundaries for consistency
- orchestration/templates/pantry.md — Review data files may need a REVIEW_TYPE marker if using conditional blocks

Acceptance criteria:
1. Each Nitpicker reviewer has explicit 'not my job' boundaries that reference the other three review types by name
2. Type-specific severity calibration is defined (what constitutes P1/P2/P3 for each type)
3. Big Head deduplication load is reduced — fewer cross-type duplicate findings at source
4. Shared concerns (report format, output structure, messaging guidelines) remain in one place, not duplicated across 4 files

title: Sentinel-file completion protocol for background subagents
description:
When the queen spawns background subagents (run_in_background: true), the only way to retrieve results is TaskOutput, which returns the raw JSONL session transcript — dumping thousands of tokens of hook progress events, tool results, and assistant turns into the queen's context window. This was observed in session 795d29 where Pest Control DMVDC and a dirt pusher were spawned in parallel as background tasks, and TaskOutput injected massive JSONL into the queen's context.

Root cause: TaskOutput returns the full session transcript for background Task agents, not just the agent's final message. Foreground Task calls don't have this problem (they return only the final message), but foreground-only prevents the queen from overlapping work (e.g., preparing next-wave prompts while dirt pushers run).

Interim mitigation: CLAUDE.md currently has a blanket prohibition on run_in_background for the queen. This prevents context leakage but sacrifices the ability to overlap work (e.g., preparing next-wave prompts while dirt pushers run). This bead replaces that blanket ban with a targeted sentinel-file protocol.

Fix: Implement a sentinel-file completion protocol:

1. Session dir setup adds a signals/ directory: mkdir -p ${SESSION_DIR}/{task-metadata,previews,prompts,signals}
2. Agent skeleton template adds a final step: "As your absolute last action, write your verdict summary to {SESSION_DIR}/signals/{TASK_SUFFIX}.done"
3. The .done file contains the agent's compact verdict (PASS/FAIL, file paths, commit hash) — the queen's clean result without JSONL
4. Queen polls for completion via Bash (stat on output file for activity, ls for .done file) — no TaskOutput, no Read on output_file
5. Inactive session + no sentinel = agent crashed, respawn

Changes needed:
- orchestration/RULES.md: Add signals/ to session dir setup, add polling protocol, update anti-patterns
- orchestration/templates/dirt-pusher-skeleton.md: Add sentinel write as final step
- CLAUDE.md: Replace blanket run_in_background prohibition with "NEVER use TaskOutput or Read on background agent output files — poll for sentinel files instead"
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md: Pest Control agents also need sentinel writes

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Session dir setup includes signals/ subdirectory
- [ ] dirt-pusher-skeleton.md includes sentinel file write as final step with verdict format
- [ ] CLAUDE.md blanket run_in_background ban replaced with targeted prohibition on TaskOutput/Read of output files, sentinel polling documented
- [ ] RULES.md documents the polling protocol (stat for activity, ls for .done, crash detection)
- [ ] Pest Control and other support agent skeletons include sentinel file write
- [ ] Queen never calls TaskOutput — verified by grep across orchestration templates

CLAUDE.md has steps that RULES.md omits:
- File issues for remaining work
- Run quality gates
- Review-findings gate (present P1 findings to user)
- Update issue status
- Verify (all changes committed AND pushed)
- Hand off (provide context for next session)

Root cause: The two files evolved independently. CLAUDE.md was extended with operational steps (quality gates, issue management) that RULES.md does not cover, while RULES.md has documentation and verification steps that CLAUDE.md does not include.

Current behavior: A Queen following CLAUDE.md's landing checklist would skip the CHANGELOG documentation commit (RULES.md Step 4) and cross-reference verification (RULES.md Step 5). The false annotation creates confusion about which file is authoritative.

Fix:
1. Update CLAUDE.md:54 annotation to "(Corresponds to RULES.md Steps 4-6.)"
2. Add missing RULES.md steps to CLAUDE.md checklist: documentation commit, cross-reference verification
3. Add missing CLAUDE.md steps to RULES.md: quality gates, issue status updates, git status verification
4. Ensure both files produce the same outcome when followed independently

Changes needed:
- CLAUDE.md:54: Fix annotation
- CLAUDE.md Landing section: Add documentation commit and cross-reference verification steps
- orchestration/RULES.md Steps 4-6: Add quality gates, issue management, git status verification

title: fix: document artifact naming convention transition point for historical sessions
description:
checkpoints.md:27-33 documents the current artifact naming convention: task-specific `pc-{TASK_SUFFIX}-{checkpoint}-{timestamp}.md` and session-wide `pc-session-{checkpoint}-{timestamp}.md` with timestamp format YYYYMMDD-HHmmss (UTC).

Older sessions use different conventions:
- _session-cd9866: Wave-based naming with no timestamps (`pc-session-cco-impl-wave1.md`, `pc-session-wwd-wave2.md`)
- _session-7edaafbb: Mixed — session-scoped DMVDC (`pc-session-dmvdc-*.md`), wave-scoped (`pc-wave2-dmvdc-*.md`), and non-standard compound names (`pc-session-dmvdc-review-fixes-*.md`)
- _session-068ecc83 (most recent): Follows the documented convention correctly

Root cause: The naming convention evolved over sessions. checkpoints.md documents the current standard but does not acknowledge that historical sessions used different formats.

Current behavior: Anyone auditing or recovering historical sessions will find artifacts that do not match the documented naming convention. This is not a bug — it is expected historical variation — but the lack of documentation causes confusion.

Fix: Add a note to checkpoints.md documenting the transition point and historical naming patterns.

Changes needed:
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md: Add note near naming convention section that sessions prior to _session-068ecc83 used wave-based naming

fix: checkpoints.md describes PC spawning code-reviewer but pest-control agent lacks Task tool
description:
Every checkpoint section in orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md (lines 17, 113, 191, 266, 339, 413, 504, 614) describes a two-layer spawn architecture: "Pest Control is the orchestrator — the Queen spawns it to run a checkpoint. Pest Control then spawns a code-reviewer agent to execute the actual checks." Each section includes "Agent type (spawned by Pest Control): code-reviewer."

Root cause: The agents/pest-control.md:4 frontmatter declares `tools: Bash, Read, Write, Glob, Grep` — no Task tool. Without the Task tool, Pest Control cannot spawn any subagent. The documented two-layer architecture (PC spawns code-reviewer) is architecturally impossible given PC tool permissions. Additionally, code-reviewer.md exists only at ~/.claude/agents/code-reviewer.md (user global), not in the repo agents/ directory, so sync-to-claude.sh does not deploy it to new environments.

Current behavior: In practice, Pest Control executes checkpoint logic directly using its Bash/Read/Glob/Grep tools — it never spawns a code-reviewer. The documentation describes an aspirational design that was never implemented. An agent reading checkpoints.md literally would attempt a spawn and fail at the tool boundary.

Fix (choose one path):
Option A (recommended — align docs to reality): Remove all "Pest Control spawns a code-reviewer" framing from checkpoints.md. Replace with direct execution language. Update the Pest Control Overview (lines 13-24) to describe PC as the executor, not the orchestrator-of-an-executor. Remove "Agent type (spawned by Pest Control)" fields from all 7 checkpoint sections.

Option B (align reality to docs): Add `Task` to pest-control.md tools frontmatter. Move code-reviewer.md into the repo agents/ directory so sync-to-claude.sh deploys it. Verify Pest Control actually spawns code-reviewer at runtime.

Changes needed:
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md: Lines 17, 113, 191, 266, 339, 413, 504, 614 — rewrite spawn language OR keep it and fix the agent
- agents/pest-control.md: Add Task tool (Option B only)
- agents/code-reviewer.md: Move from ~/.claude/agents/ to repo agents/ (Option B only)

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] checkpoints.md spawn architecture matches pest-control.md tool permissions
- [ ] No reference to "spawns a code-reviewer" if PC lacks Task tool
- [ ] If code-reviewer is retained, it exists in repo agents/ directory (not just ~/.claude/)
- [ ] RULES.md Agent Types table reflects any agent changes

Add instrumented dummy reviewer via tmux for context usage measurement
description:
Root cause: We have no empirical data on how much context window reviewers consume during a review cycle. If total changed file volume pushes a reviewer past 70-80% of the 200k context limit, conversation compaction silently degrades review quality. Without measurement data, any planning-time file budget would be a guess.

Fix: Spawn an additional dummy reviewer as a tmux window alongside the Nitpicker team for the purpose of measuring context usage. The dummy reviewer:

- Mirrors the correctness reviewer (heaviest context load — reads acceptance criteria against actual code for every task)
- Receives the same data file and reads the same changed files
- Follows the same review template and writes a report
- Runs in its own tmux pane so the user can observe context usage via the Claude Code UI
- Its report is NOT wired into Big Head's consolidation — findings are discarded

Note: This task uses tmux within iTerm2's control mode (tmux -CC). See the prerequisite research task (ant-farm-lajv) for the exact invocation commands. Standard tmux send-keys may behave differently in control mode.

The user manually records per session:
- Number of files the reviewer was asked to read
- Total line count across those files (from bead file references)
- Observed context usage percentage (from Claude Code UI)
- Whether compaction occurred (yes/no)
- At what point compaction occurred (during file reading, during report writing, or not at all)

This produces a reliable mapping from file/line counts to context usage percentage, which informs the planning-time review budget for the Scout and meta-scout.

Sunset clause: Remove after 30 sessions of data collection or when a confident file-budget threshold is established, whichever comes first.

Affected files:
- orchestration/RULES.md — Add step to spawn dummy reviewer tmux pane during review phase
- orchestration/templates/pantry.md — Compose a data file for the dummy reviewer (clone of correctness data file)

Acceptance criteria:
1. Dummy reviewer spawns as a tmux window (using iTerm2-compatible tmux control mode commands from prerequisite task) during the review phase
2. Dummy reviewer receives identical input to the correctness reviewer
3. Big Head does not read or consolidate the dummy reviewer's report
4. User can observe context usage in the dummy reviewer's tmux pane
5. After data collection period, the dummy reviewer can be removed without affecting the rest of the review workflow

title: Research tmux + iTerm2 control mode integration for spawning Claude Code sessions
description:
Root cause: The meta-orchestration design and dummy reviewer instrumentation both require spawning Claude Code sessions in tmux windows that are visible and interactive within iTerm2. iTerm2 has native tmux integration but requires control mode (tmux -CC) rather than standard tmux invocation. The exact commands for creating windows, sending keystrokes, and monitoring panes within iTerm2's control mode may differ from standard tmux usage.

Fix: Research the correct invocation patterns for:
- Starting a tmux session in iTerm2 control mode (tmux -CC)
- Creating new tmux windows that appear as native iTerm2 tabs
- Sending commands to specific windows (tmux send-keys equivalent in control mode)
- Monitoring whether a window's process is still running
- Any iTerm2-specific gotchas (startup timing, key encoding, session attachment)

This is an internet research task — search iTerm2 docs, tmux control mode docs, and community resources rather than trial-and-error.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Document the exact commands needed to: start a tmux control mode session, create a new window, send a prompt to that window, and check window status — all within iTerm2
2. Verify whether tmux send-keys works as expected in control mode or if an alternative is needed
3. Update the dummy reviewer bead's description with the correct iTerm2-compatible commands for launching the dummy reviewer
4. Update the meta-orchestration plan (docs/plans/2026-02-19-meta-orchestration-plan.md) tmux examples with correct iTerm2 control mode commands

description:

PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md defines three tiers:
- Tier 1: {UPPERCASE} — Queen-provided at spawn time
- Tier 2: {lowercase-kebab} — agent-derived at runtime
- Tier 3: ${SHELL_VAR} — bash interpreter

The double-brace {{SLOT}} syntax is a fourth tier substituted by shell scripts (fill-review-slots.sh, compose-review-skeletons.sh) between Queen composition and agent delivery. It is not documented in PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md. Additionally, the File-by-File Audit table in that file marks reviews.md as PASS with "None" for all tiers — factually incorrect since reviews.md uses {{REVIEW_ROUND}}.

Root cause: The double-brace convention was introduced by the review slot-filling scripts without updating the placeholder conventions document.

Fix: Add Tier 4 to PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md and correct the audit table.

Changes needed:
- orchestration/templates/PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md: Add Tier 4 (Script-Substituted {{DOUBLE_BRACE}}) with explanation
- orchestration/templates/PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md: Fix File-by-File Audit table row for reviews.md

Acceptance Criteria:

- [ ] Tier 4 description identifies fill-review-slots.sh as the substitution mechanism

- [ ] All {{SLOT}} markers across templates are accounted for in the new tier description

title: RULES.md Step 3b is too dense for reliable agent parsing under pressure
description:
Root cause: RULES.md:97-127 Step 3b (Review) packs ~30 lines of unbroken prose with embedded code, bullet lists, and conditional logic (round 1 vs round 2+). This is the most cognitively demanding step in RULES.md and requires reading the entire block to find specific sub-steps like 'how to spawn round 2+ team'.

Fix: Break Step 3b into labeled sub-steps (e.g., 'Step 3b-i: Gather inputs', 'Step 3b-ii: Fill slots', 'Step 3b-iii: Spawn reviewers (round-dependent)').

Acceptance criteria:

title: fix: review-skeletons/ and review-reports/ missing from Step 0 session directory setup
description:
The Step 0 session directory setup in orchestration/RULES.md:336 creates 5 subdirectories:
`mkdir -p "${SESSION_DIR}"/{task-metadata,previews,prompts,pc,summaries}`

Two additional directories appear in every session that reaches the review phase but are not listed:
- review-skeletons/ — created by compose-review-skeletons.sh (called by Pantry in Step 2)
- review-reports/ — created by mkdir -p at RULES.md:175 (Step 3b-iii)

Root cause: These directories were introduced after the original Step 0 setup was written. They are created lazily by their respective workflow phases, but the Session Directory section gives no hint they will exist.

Current behavior: The Step 0 mkdir command is treated as the complete session directory setup. A reader has an incomplete mental model. Crash recovery between Step 2 and Step 3b could fail if it expects these directories to exist before the lazy-creation step runs.

Fix: Add a note after RULES.md:336 documenting the additional directories created during later phases. Do NOT add them to the mkdir command (lazy creation is intentional — sessions that never reach reviews should not have empty review dirs).

Changes needed:
- orchestration/RULES.md: Add note after line 336 listing review-skeletons/ and review-reports/ with their creation points

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] RULES.md Session Directory section documents all 7 subdirectories that appear in practice
- [ ] Note clarifies lazy creation (review dirs created by their respective phases, not at Step 0)
- [ ] Crash recovery documentation accounts for directories that may not yet exist

description:
Root cause: RULES.md Step 0 (line 86/115) creates the prompts/ directory via brace expansion in mkdir. pantry.md Review Mode Step 3 (line 107/110) also says 'Create the prompts directory if needed.' title: fix: DMVDC Nitpicker artifact naming in checkpoints.md does not match actual filenames
description:
checkpoints.md:475 documents DMVDC Nitpicker artifact naming as `pc-{TASK_SUFFIX}-dmvdc-review-{timestamp}.md` with example TASK_SUFFIX values like `review-clarity`, `review-edge` (line 478).

Actual filenames in _session-068ecc83/pc/:
- pc-review-correctness-dmvdc-20260221-182700.md
- pc-review-edge-cases-dmvdc-20260221-182700.md

Two deviations from the documented format:
1. The `-review` suffix after `dmvdc` is absent in actual files (documented: `...-dmvdc-review-{ts}`, actual: `...-dmvdc-{ts}`)
2. The TASK_SUFFIX values used are bare review type names (`correctness`, `edge-cases`) rather than the documented compound `review-{type}` (`review-clarity`, `review-edge`)

Root cause: The naming convention in checkpoints.md was written speculatively and never validated against actual Pest Control output.

Current behavior: Anyone querying pc/ for the documented pattern `*dmvdc-review*` will find zero matches. The actual pattern is `*-dmvdc-{timestamp}*` with the review type before `dmvdc`.

Fix: Update checkpoints.md:475 to match actual convention and update example values on line 478.

Changes needed:
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md:475: Change to `pc-{review-type}-dmvdc-{timestamp}.md`
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md:478: Update example values to `correctness`, `edge-cases`, `clarity`, `excellence`

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] checkpoints.md DMVDC Nitpicker naming matches actual artifact filenames
- [ ] Example TASK_SUFFIX values match actual Nitpicker review type names
- [ ] Querying pc/ with the documented pattern finds actual files

title: Add pre-flight Scout strategy verification checkpoint via haiku PC agent
description:
Root cause: The Scout's execution strategy (wave groupings, task-to-wave assignments, file conflict analysis) is currently validated only by human approval. This creates a bottleneck for automation and doesn't catch mechanical errors like file/task mismatches or intra-wave dependency violations that a human reviewer might miss.

Fix: Add a lightweight haiku-model Pest Control checkpoint that runs automatically after the Scout produces its strategy. This verifies the strategy's correctness through three mechanical checks before any Queens or dirt-pushers are spawned.

Verification checks (all are set comparisons, ideal for haiku):

1. No file overlaps within a wave: For each wave, collect all affected files from all tasks in that wave. If any file appears in two or more tasks within the same wave, FAIL with details.

2. File lists match bead descriptions: For each task in the strategy, compare the Scout's reported affected files against the bead's actual affected files (from bd show). Mismatch → FAIL.

3. No intra-wave dependency violations: For each wave, check that no task in wave N depends on (is blocked by) another task in wave N. Dependencies must flow across waves, not within them.

Flow:
1. Scout produces strategy with wave plan
2. Haiku PC agent reads strategy + beads for all tasks in the plan
3. Runs all three checks
4. PASS → proceed to Pantry/implementation. FAIL → re-run Scout or escalate to user.

This checkpoint is valuable independently of meta-orchestration — it hardens the existing single-Queen workflow by catching Scout errors before they cascade into implementation. In a meta-orchestration context, it replaces the human approval gate entirely.

Model: haiku (mechanical set comparisons, no judgment needed)
Agent type: pest-control or dedicated pre-flight-pc variant

Affected files:
- orchestration/templates/checkpoints.md — Add new checkpoint definition (Scout Strategy Verification / SSV)
- orchestration/RULES.md — Add SSV checkpoint after Scout returns, before Pantry spawns
- Potentially a new skeleton template for the SSV PC agent

Acceptance criteria:
1. Haiku PC agent runs after Scout returns and before Pantry is spawned
2. All three checks (file overlap, file list match, dependency ordering) are performed
3. PASS allows workflow to continue without human approval
4. FAIL halts workflow and reports specific violations (which tasks, which files, which dependencies)
5. Checkpoint report written to {session-dir}/pc/pc-session-ssv-{timestamp}.md

title: Session ID collision risk from truncated shasum with epoch-second input
description:
Root cause: RULES.md line 85 generates session IDs via 'date +%s | shasum | head -c 6' which produces a 6-char hex string. The input is epoch seconds, so two Queens started within the same second would produce identical session IDs, causing artifact directory collisions.

Affected surfaces:
- RULES.md:85 -- Session ID generation command

Contributing reviews: Edge Cases (Finding 1, P3)
Single reviewer finding. P3 because concurrent Queens in the same repo are already implied to be unsupported by concurrency rules.

Fix: Either add per-invocation randomness (e.g., '(date +%s%N; echo $$) | shasum | head -c 6') or document that concurrent Queens are unsupported.

Acceptance criteria:
- Either the session ID has sufficient entropy to avoid same-second collisions, or the single-Queen constraint is explicitly documented
close_reason: Fixed: quoted SESSION_DIR in all mkdir commands across RULES.md, reviews.md, and PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md. Added PID entropy to session ID generation.

title: Polling loop in reviews.md Step 0a uses fragile wc -l line-counting with globs
description: The polling loop in reviews.md Step 0a (lines 370-383) uses a fragile pattern: it chains 4 ls commands with && into a MISSING_REPORTS variable, then checks wc -l equals 4 to determine all reports are present. Problems: (1) Variable name is inverted -- it stores found files, not missing ones. (2) If a glob matches multiple files (e.g., from a re-run), wc -l exceeds 4 and the loop never breaks, silently timing out. (3) No post-loop check distinguishes timeout from success -- the script falls through without error handling. (4) Shell state does not persist between Bash tool calls in Claude Code, so the loop variables would reset if Big Head splits execution across calls. Affected surfaces: reviews.md:370-383. Fix: Replace wc -l counting with individual [ -f ] checks per exact filename. Add post-loop timeout detection. Add comment requiring single-invocation execution.

title: fill-review-slots.sh accepts review round 0 as valid input
description:
Root cause: fill-review-slots.sh:80 validates review round with regex '^[0-9]+$' which accepts 0. The review system uses 1-based rounds. Round 0 would silently fall into the round 2+ branch (only correctness + edge-cases), skipping clarity and excellence reviews.

Affected surfaces:
- scripts/fill-review-slots.sh:78-83

Found by: Edge Cases (P3 - F4), Correctness (P3 - C3)
Both identified the same validation gap at the same line.

Fix: Change regex to '^[1-9][0-9]*$' or add explicit check for round >= 1.

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Round 0 is rejected with an error message
- [ ] Round 1 and higher continue to work

title: fix: one-TeamCreate-per-session constraint undocumented in operator-facing docs
description:
Claude Code supports only one TeamCreate call per session. This constraint shapes the entire review architecture: Pest Control is included as a Nitpicker team member (rather than spawned separately after the team completes) because a second TeamCreate for a standalone PC spawn would fail at runtime.

The constraint is documented only in MEMORY.md:19-21: "Claude Code only supports one TeamCreate per session. The Nitpicker team (4 reviewers + Big Head) uses this slot. QA coordination (Pantry ↔ Pest Control) must use file-based handoff, not teams."

Root cause: The constraint was discovered empirically and captured in MEMORY.md but never propagated to RULES.md, CLAUDE.md, or CONTRIBUTING.md.

Current behavior: An operator extending the workflow (e.g., trying to create a second team for QA coordination) will encounter a runtime failure with no prior warning in any authoritative doc. The architectural rationale for why PC is inside the Nitpicker team is invisible to anyone reading RULES.md.

Fix: Document the constraint in RULES.md near the Nitpicker team setup.

Changes needed:
- orchestration/RULES.md Step 3b-iv (near team setup): Add note explaining one-TeamCreate-per-session constraint and why PC is a team member

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] RULES.md documents the one-TeamCreate-per-session constraint
- [ ] Note explains the architectural implication (PC must be team member, not separate spawn)
- [ ] CONTRIBUTING.md or SETUP.md mentions the constraint for framework extenders

title: Templates lack failure artifact specification for error paths
description: Root cause: Multiple templates specify FAIL conditions but do not specify what artifact to write on failure. Big Head Step 0 (reviews.md:349-352) says 'Do NOT proceed' when reports missing but does not instruct writing a failure artifact. Downstream consumers (Queen, CCB) have no written record of the failure, making state recovery ambiguous. Related to existing beads ant-farm-e9k and ant-farm-zeu. Fix: Establish convention that when any template reaches a FAIL condition, it writes a brief artifact to the expected output path explaining the failure. Acceptance criteria: (1) Big Head Step 0 writes failure artifact when reports missing. (2) Convention documented for all templates. Found by: edge-cases-reviewer (P2).

title: Nested markdown code fences in reviews.md error return template break rendering
description: The error return template in reviews.md Step 0a (lines 390-420) contains a nested markdown code fence. The outer fence opens at line 390 with triple backticks. The inner fence at lines 414-416 (for the re-spawn instruction) uses the same triple backticks, which prematurely closes the outer fence in standard markdown rendering. Lines 417-420 render as regular text instead of inside the code block. An agent parsing this template literally could misinterpret the template boundaries. Affected surfaces: reviews.md:414-420. Fix: Use quadruple backticks for the outer fence, or indent the inner block with 4+ spaces, or use tildes for the inner fence.

title: [Review RC-3] Stale _archive/ files persist after rsync --delete removal
description: P2: Removing --delete from rsync in sync-to-claude.sh without adding --exclude='_archive/' leaves previously-synced deprecated instruction files at ~/.claude/orchestration/_archive/ where agents using glob patterns could read them. Fix: add --exclude='_archive/' to the rsync command.

title: awk sub() in fill_slot treats & and \ as special in replacement values
description:
Root cause: fill-review-slots.sh:176 uses awk `sub(slot, val)` for text replacement. POSIX awk's sub() interprets `&` in the replacement string as 'insert matched text' and `\` as an escape character. If any slot value (file path, commit range) contains these characters, the substitution silently corrupts output.

Affected surfaces:
- fill-review-slots.sh:151-183 (fill_slot function, specifically line 176)

Found by: Edge Cases (P2), Correctness (P3 - 'theoretical')
Edge Cases rated P2 because it is a latent corruption risk in production code. Correctness rated P3 because current inputs are unlikely to contain & or \. Taking the higher severity: P2.

Fix: After reading val from the temp file in the awk script, escape special chars:
```awk
gsub(/\\/, "&&", val)   # escape backslashes first
gsub(/&/, "\\&", val)   # then escape ampersands
```

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Slot values containing & and \ are substituted literally
- [ ] Existing slot values (paths, timestamps, task IDs) continue to work unchanged

title: [Review RC-1] extract_agent_section awk pattern requires 2 delimiters but skeletons have 1

This silently produces empty agent instruction bodies, breaking the review pipeline. Fix: revert to count>=1 (skip first ---) or add a second --- to skeleton files.

title: MANDATORY GATE style inconsistency in reviews.md
Bead Backlog Audit: Triage 176 Open Issues Against Current Codebase
description:
176 open beads exist, mostly P3 bugs filed during documentation drift audits (sessions 068ecc83, db790c8d, e7ff7c0d). The codebase has changed significantly since filing — commits 1b0037e (review script merger), 3f52803 (parse-progress-log fix), 17a6e03 (checkpoints Pest Control correction), 7569c5e (model reference update), and others have modified the exact files these beads reference.

Current state: No systematic triage has been done. Some beads are likely already fixed, some are duplicated (16 known exact-title pairs), and some may be irrelevant (e.g., beads about archived pantry-review.md). Without triage, the backlog is noisy and makes it hard to identify genuinely actionable work.

Architecture: Three-pass approach with file-scoped batching.
- Pass 0: Mechanical pre-processing — export, exact-title dedup, partition 168 non-epic beads into 9 file-scoped batches
- Pass 1: 9 parallel verification agents — each reads its batch of beads + the relevant codebase files, assigns a verdict per bead (STILL_VALID, ALREADY_FIXED, IRRELEVANT, NEEDS_CONTEXT, DUPLICATE_SUSPECT)
- Pass 2: 1 consolidation agent — merges all batch outputs into a unified triage report with duplicate clusters, epic health summaries, and priority re-calibration suggestions

All artifacts are stored in .beads/agent-summaries/_session-39adef65/audit/.

Success Criteria:
- [ ] Every open bead (176) has exactly one triage verdict
- [ ] Duplicate clusters are identified and resolved (keep/close recommendations)
- [ ] Final triage report is produced as audit/final-triage-report.md
- [ ] User reviews and approves report before any bd close commands run
- [ ] Net backlog reduction is documented with before/after counts

title: Verification Pipeline no defined precedence when Big Head and CCB checks disagree

description:
Root cause: fill-review-slots.sh:238 uses `sed '$d'` to 'remove trailing newline' from the expected_paths list, but `sed '$d'` deletes the LAST LINE, not a trailing newline character. This silently drops the last review type's report path from Big Head's expected paths list.

Impact: In round 1, excellence-review path is dropped (3 of 4 emitted). In round 2+, edge-cases-review path is dropped (1 of 2 emitted). Big Head cannot verify all reports exist before starting consolidation.

Affected surfaces:

Found by: Correctness (P1), Edge Cases (P2), Clarity (P3 - comment mismatch), Excellence (P3 - fragility)
All 4 reviewers independently identified this as an issue, confirming it is the highest-priority finding.

Fix: Replace `sed '$d'` with a loop that builds the list without a trailing newline, or use `sed '/^$/d'` to strip blank lines.

Acceptance criteria:

- [ ] No trailing blank line in the expected paths output

title: [Review RC-2] BSD grep \s not recognized in scrub-pii.sh verification

title: Development artifacts left in production templates (NEW annotation, CRITICAL FIX note)

PLACEHOLDER_CONVENTIONS.md does not document or validate angle-bracket placeholder syntax
Improve Scout agent type tie-breaking with deeper catalog reads and explicit fallback
description:
Root cause: When the Scout cannot clearly differentiate between agent types for a task, it falls back to returning 'group' as the agent type. This reaches the user (via the Queen's strategy presentation) as an opaque label with no information about which types were tied or why. The user has no actionable way to break the tie.

The underlying issue is that the Scout's agent catalog (Step 2.5) only reads YAML frontmatter — one sentence of description per agent. This is intentionally lightweight but provides insufficient signal for 'description match' (selection criterion 3), making ties more likely than necessary.

Fix (two-pronged):

1. Deeper catalog reads on tie: When the Scout's four-step selection criteria (file extension, task nature, description match, fallback) produce a tie between two or more agent types, the Scout should read the full agent MD files for ONLY the tied candidates. This keeps context cost proportional to the ambiguity while giving the Scout enough signal to differentiate. If reading the full files resolves the tie, use the winner. If the tie persists after deeper reads, proceed to step 2.

2. Explicit fallback format for unresolved ties: When a tie cannot be broken, the Scout's task metadata and strategy presentation must list all tied agent types with the task title for context, not 'group'. Format in strategy output should be:
   {task-id}: {task-title} — PICK ONE: [python-pro | debugger]
This gives the user task context and the specific candidates in one line, making the tie-break decision self-contained without requiring them to look up the task.

Multiple ties across different tasks should each list their own candidates independently.

Affected files:
- orchestration/templates/scout.md — Step 2.5 catalog build (add deep-read-on-tie logic), Step 3 agent type selection (update tie handling), Step 5 strategy presentation (update format for tied types)

Acceptance criteria:
1. Scout reads full agent MD files for tied candidates (and only tied candidates) before falling back
2. Unresolved ties surface in strategy as '{task-id}: {task-title} — PICK ONE: [type-a | type-b]' instead of 'group'
3. Each task with a tie lists its own candidates independently
4. No increase in Scout context usage when there are no ties (frontmatter-only reads remain the default)

title: fix: installed pre-push hook is fatal on sync failure, contradicting install-hooks.sh non-fatal design
description:
The installed git hook at .git/hooks/pre-push runs sync-to-claude.sh under `set -euo pipefail` with no error handling (line 11: `"$SYNC_SCRIPT"`). Any sync failure exits non-zero and blocks the push.

The hook template in scripts/install-hooks.sh (lines 50-57) wraps the sync call in a non-fatal check: `if ! "$SYNC_SCRIPT"; then echo WARNING ... fi` — sync failure prints a warning but exits 0, allowing the push to proceed. The comment at line 50 explains the design intent: "Sync failure is intentionally non-fatal: the push should never be blocked by a local sync tool."

Root cause: The installed hook is an older version that was not regenerated after install-hooks.sh was updated to make sync failure non-fatal. The two files have diverged: the installed hook enforces fatal sync, while the installer generates non-fatal sync.

Current behavior: A developer pushing code will experience a blocked push if sync-to-claude.sh fails for any reason (disk full, permission error, rsync failure). This contradicts the documented design intent. Running `./scripts/install-hooks.sh` would fix the installed hook, but until re-run, every push is at risk of being blocked by a transient sync error.

Fix:
1. Re-run `./scripts/install-hooks.sh` to replace the installed hook with the current non-fatal version
2. Add a note to CONTRIBUTING.md reminding contributors to re-run install-hooks.sh after pulling changes to that script
3. Consider adding a version marker to generated hooks so staleness can be detected

Also fix CONTRIBUTING.md:161 which claims rsync uses `--delete` — the actual script intentionally omits `--delete` (with an explanatory comment at lines 24-25). Update to reflect the non-delete policy and the _archive/ exclusion.

Changes needed:
- .git/hooks/pre-push: Regenerate via install-hooks.sh
- CONTRIBUTING.md:161: Fix rsync --delete claim and add _archive/ exclusion note
- CONTRIBUTING.md: Add reminder about re-running install-hooks.sh after changes

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Installed .git/hooks/pre-push matches output of install-hooks.sh (non-fatal sync)
- [ ] CONTRIBUTING.md rsync description matches actual sync-to-claude.sh behavior (no --delete, excludes _archive/)
- [ ] CONTRIBUTING.md includes guidance on re-running install-hooks.sh after pulling changes
- [ ] Push succeeds even when sync-to-claude.sh fails (manual test)

Note: {EPIC_ID} was removed from all active templates in commits dabb764/f838cef (session-scoping shift). It is no longer a variable to standardize.

title: Checkpoints CCO FAIL verdict format has no example
description: checkpoints.md shows PASS verdict format but no example of a FAIL verdict with specific check failures listed. A fresh Pest Control agent might format FAIL output incorrectly. Add a FAIL example showing check number, name, and evidence.

title: Round 2+ scope instructions inconsistently presented between nitpicker-skeleton and reviews.md
pantry-review.md (archived) tense inconsistency
compose-review-skeletons.sh no SESSION_DIR existence check before mkdir -p
scrub-pii.sh does not re-stage issues.jsonl when run standalone outside pre-commit
description: Root cause: When scrub-pii.sh runs standalone (not as pre-commit hook), it modifies issues.jsonl in place but does not re-stage it. The git add is in install-hooks.sh's generated hook, not in scrub-pii.sh itself. Undocumented manual step. Fix: Add a reminder note in the script or detect non-hook context and print 'Run git add .beads/issues.jsonl'. Found by: edge-cases-reviewer (P3).

title: checkpoints.md sampling formula contradicts 'minimum 3' prose text
description:
Root cause: checkpoints.md line 311 specifies the formula min(5, ceil(N/3)) for selecting findings to spot-check, but the same line states 'minimum 3, or all findings if fewer than 3'. The formula can produce values below 3 -- e.g., for N=6, ceil(6/3)=2, then min(5,2)=2. The English text says minimum 3 but the formula does not enforce this floor. Pest Control could either under-sample (following formula) or ignore the formula (following prose), creating verification ambiguity.

Affected surfaces:
- checkpoints.md:311 -- formula/prose contradiction in Checkpoint B Nitpickers sampling rule

Contributing reviews: Clarity (Finding 8, P3 with cross-ref to edge-cases), Edge Cases (Finding 10, P2)
Edge-cases reviewer confirmed this as a genuine behavioral risk at P2. Taking highest: P2.

Fix: Replace min(5, ceil(N/3)) with max(3, min(5, ceil(N/3))) to enforce the stated minimum of 3. Rewrite the prose in plain English: 'Pick at least 3 findings (or all findings if fewer than 3 exist), up to a maximum of 5.'

Acceptance criteria:
- The formula on line 311 enforces a floor of 3
- The English description matches the formula exactly
- No contradiction between formula and prose

title: parse-progress-log.sh misleading POSIX portability comment vs bash-only constructs
description: Root cause: scripts/parse-progress-log.sh:104-150 uses a temp-directory key-value store headed with a 'POSIX-compatible' comment, but the script freely uses bash-only constructs elsewhere (e.g. [[ =~ ]] at line 169, set -euo pipefail at line 25). The actual portability target is bash 3+ (not POSIX sh). The misleading comment could confuse future maintainers about the portability baseline. Fix: Reword the comment heading to 'Bash 3+-compatible key-value store (replaces bash 4+ declare -A). Uses a temp directory to avoid associative arrays.' Affected surfaces: scripts/parse-progress-log.sh:104-150, :169. Source: Excellence Review Finding 2 (P2), Clarity Review Finding 9 (P3).

title: Wave concept used in RULES.md but never defined
description: RULES.md Hard Gates table references 'Next agent in wave' and checkpoints.md says 'BEFORE spawning next agent in same wave'. The concept of a wave (batch of parallel agents) is never defined. Add to glossary: 'Wave: a batch of agents spawned in parallel. Wave N completes before Wave N+1 begins.'

title: Verification Pipeline Rationale has redundant 'Why both?' paragraph
AGG-031: Add data file format specification to skeleton templates
description:
Skeleton templates say Read your task context from {DATA_FILE_PATH} but never specify the file is markdown, dont list expected sections, and provide no parsing guidance. A cold agent might expect JSON or YAML.

Fix: Add a brief format note to each skeleton (e.g., The data file is markdown with sections: Context, Scope Boundaries, Focus.).

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] dirt-pusher-skeleton.md specifies the data file format and expected sections
- [ ] nitpicker-skeleton.md specifies the data file format and expected sections
- [ ] Both skeletons explicitly state the file is markdown (not JSON/YAML)

title: fix: Scout agent frontmatter declares model: sonnet, contradicting RULES.md model: opus
description:
The Scout agent definition at agents/scout-organizer.md:5 declares `model: sonnet` in its frontmatter. The Model Assignments table in orchestration/RULES.md:294 specifies `model: opus` for the Scout, stating "Every Task tool call the Queen makes MUST include the model parameter from this table."

Root cause: When the Scout was promoted from sonnet to opus (for orchestration complexity), the RULES.md Model Assignments table was updated but the agent frontmatter was not. The same stale model value propagated to GLOSSARY.md:80 and README.md:75, which both still say "sonnet."

Current behavior: If the Queen supplies `model: "opus"` as instructed by RULES.md, the frontmatter is silently overridden — no runtime failure but a false mental model. If the Queen omits the model parameter (e.g., following a partial read of instructions or during error recovery), Claude Code loads the frontmatter value and the Scout runs as sonnet instead of the intended opus. The frontmatter is the fallback, so this is a latent S1.

Fix: Update the model field in all three locations to match RULES.md:
1. agents/scout-organizer.md:5 — change `model: sonnet` to `model: opus`
2. orchestration/GLOSSARY.md:80 — change Scout row model from `sonnet` to `opus`
3. README.md:75 — change "a sonnet subagent" to "an opus subagent"

Also fix the Pantry model in GLOSSARY.md:81 (says sonnet, RULES.md:295 says opus).

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] agents/scout-organizer.md frontmatter says `model: opus`
- [ ] orchestration/GLOSSARY.md Scout row says `opus`
- [ ] orchestration/GLOSSARY.md Pantry row says `opus`
- [ ] README.md Scout description says "opus" not "sonnet"
- [ ] No other files reference Scout or Pantry as sonnet-tier agents (grep verification)

Audit reference: DRIFT-001 + DRIFT-017 from docs/audits/2026-02-21-orchestration-drift-audit.md

title: fix: README shows 5-member Nitpicker team but RULES.md requires 6 (Pest Control inside team)
description:
README.md:59 describes the Nitpicker team as "4 reviewers + Big Head" (5 members). README.md:218 shows the flow: "create Nitpicker team (4 reviewers + Big Head)" with the Queen spawning a separate Pest Control after the team completes (line 201).

RULES.md:179 specifies a 6-member team: "Round 1: 6 members — 4 reviewers + Big Head + Pest Control." RULES.md:182 explains why: "Pest Control MUST be a team member so Big Head can SendMessage to it." RULES.md:184 confirms: "After team completes, DMVDC and CCB have already run inside the team."

Root cause: README was written before Pest Control was added as a team member. The architectural change (driven by the one-TeamCreate-per-session constraint — see DRIFT-015) was applied to RULES.md but not propagated to the README.

Current behavior: An operator following the README Step 3b flow would spawn a 5-member team and then separately spawn Pest Control. Big Head would be unable to SendMessage to PC (different team/no team), breaking the team-internal DMVDC/CCB flow.

Fix: Update README architecture diagram, team description, and flow diagram to reflect 6-member team with PC inside.

Changes needed:
- README.md:59: Change to "4 reviewers + Big Head + Pest Control"
- README.md:218: Update flow diagram to show 6-member team
- README.md:201: Remove separate PC spawn; note DMVDC/CCB run inside team

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] README describes 6-member Nitpicker team (4 reviewers + Big Head + Pest Control)
- [ ] Flow diagram shows PC as team member, not separate spawn
- [ ] No reference to spawning PC separately after team completes

Audit reference: DRIFT-016 from docs/audits/2026-02-21-orchestration-drift-audit.md

title: fix: SSV checkpoint missing from RULES.md Model Assignments table
description:
The Model Assignments table in orchestration/RULES.md:288-304 lists model assignments for all Pest Control checkpoint types (CCO, WWD, DMVDC, CCB) but has no row for SSV (Scout Strategy Verification). The table header at RULES.md:290 states: "Every Task tool call the Queen makes MUST include the model parameter from this table. Omitting model causes the agent to inherit the Queen's opus model, wasting tokens on agents that don't need it."

Root cause: When SSV was added as a checkpoint, it was documented inline in Step 1b (RULES.md:89 — `spawn Pest Control (pest-control, model: "haiku") for Scout Strategy Verification`) and in checkpoints.md:612 (`Model: haiku`), but the Model Assignments table was not updated.

Current behavior: A Queen following the table as the authoritative model reference will find no SSV entry and must hunt in Step 1b prose. Three independent audit agents flagged this same gap.

Fix: Add a row to the Model Assignments table between the current first and second rows:
`| PC — SSV | Task (pest-control) | haiku | Pure set comparisons — no judgment required |`

Changes needed:
- orchestration/RULES.md:297 (insert new row in Model Assignments table)

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Model Assignments table includes PC — SSV row with model haiku
- [ ] All 5 PC checkpoint types (SSV, CCO, WWD, DMVDC, CCB) have table entries
- [ ] Table row note matches checkpoints.md:612 rationale

Audit reference: DRIFT-005 from docs/audits/2026-02-21-orchestration-drift-audit.md

title: Pantry fail-fast says Halt but behavior is skip-to-next-task
description: Line 30 of pantry.md says 'FAIL-FAST CHECK: Halt and report for any of these conditions.' The word 'Halt' implies stopping all processing. But the actual behavior in Conditions 1-3 is per-task skipping: 'Do not proceed with task brief composition for this task' and 'skip to the next task.' The Pantry continues processing remaining tasks after a failure. The 'Halt' wording became more visibly misleading after ant-farm-zeu added detailed per-condition behavior (write artifact, skip to next). Affected surfaces: pantry.md:30-31. Fix: Change 'Halt and report' to 'Check and skip on failure' or 'Validate before proceeding.'

title: Explore tmux-based agent spawning for real-time observability
description: Explore running orchestrated subagents in tmux windows instead of background Task agents, so you can observe agent work in real-time. Key research questions: (1) Can the boss-bot launch claude CLI sessions in tmux windows via Bash? (2) How to pipe prompts into those sessions? (3) How to collect results back? (4) Hook-based automation options? Goal: Each spawned agent gets its own tmux window you can switch to with ctrl+b n/p. May influence orchestration templates if feasible. (Moved from Documentation Gaps epic ant-farm-9wq)

title: RULES.md round 2+ composition phrasing inconsistency with reviews.md
description: RULES.md:171-177 says (Correctness + Edge Cases) for round 2+ but reviews.md explicitly notes Clarity and Excellence are dropped. Minor wording inconsistency. Fix: align RULES.md phrasing. Consolidated from: Excellence F7.

title: Stale pantry-review agent on disk unguarded by Scout exclusion list
description: Root cause: Commit ant-farm-oc9v removed pantry-review from the Scout exclusion list at orchestration/templates/scout.md:63, but sync-to-claude.sh never deletes files from ~/.claude/agents/. The stale ~/.claude/agents/pantry-review.md remains on disk. Without the exclusion list entry, the Scout could recommend pantry-review as a Dirt Pusher for tasks matching its description, producing a silent failure (orchestration agent spawned as implementation agent, yielding no code and no commit). Fix: Re-add pantry-review to the exclusion list at scout.md:63 until users have confirmed manual deletion of ~/.claude/agents/pantry-review.md. Affected surface: orchestration/templates/scout.md:63. Source: Edge Cases Review Finding 1.

title: Template-to-agent communication gaps: unactionable escalation path and unfilled placeholder
